nameMeanings.name: Brocton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: habitational name from Broxton in Cheshire, an obscure name, possibly from Old English burgæsn ‘burial place’.
habitational name from Browston in Suffolk, recorded in Domesday Book as Brockestuna, from the Old English personal name Brocc (from Old English brocc ‘badger’) + Old English tūn ‘settlement’.

variants.name: Broxson
meanings: English (Staffordshire): variant of   Broxton  . This surname is rare in Britain.

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

variants.name: George
meanings: Via Old French and Latin, from Greek Georgios (a derivative of geōrgos ‘farmer’, from gē ‘earth’ + ergein ‘to work’). This was the name of several early saints, including the shadowy figure who is now the patron of England (as well as of Germany and Portugal). If the saint existed at all, he was perhaps martyred in Palestine in the persecutions of Christians instigated by the Emperor Diocletian at the beginning of the 4th century. The popular legend in which the hero slays a dragon is a medieval Italian invention. He was for a long time a more important saint in the Orthodox Church than in the West, and the name was not much used in England during the Middle Ages, even after St George came to be regarded as the patron of England in the 14th century. Its use increased from the 1400s, and by 1500 it was regularly among the most popular male names. This popularity was reinforced when George I came to the throne in 1714 , bringing this name with him from Germany. It has been one of the most popular English boys' names ever since.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir William Brocton Sr was born in 1388, in Stanton Long, Shropshire, England, United Kingdom, his father, John Brocton, was 23 and his mother, Isabell Collins, was 21. He had at least 1 son with Lady Brocton. He died in his hometown.

ko.lifeSummary: Sir William Brocton Sr(이)가 1388년 영국 잉글랜드 슈럽실르 스탠턴 론그에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 John Brocton(은)는 23세였고, 그의 어머니 Isabell Collins(은)는 21세였다. 그는 Lady Brocton(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 그의 고향에서 사망했다.
